content:
Calculate the work done in the oxidation of one mole $\mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, according to reaction.

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})} \\ & \left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \end{aligned} $$
explanation:
Step 1: Recall formula for work done

When reactions involve a change in number of moles of gases:

$ w = -\Delta n_{\text{gas}} \, R T $

Here, $ \Delta n_{\text{gas}} = n_{\text{products, gas}} - n_{\text{reactants, gas}},$

$T = 27 + 273 = 300 \,\text{K},$

$R = 8.314 \,\text{J mol}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-1}.$

Step 2: Calculate Δn for the given reaction

Reactants (gases): $4 \text{ HCl} + 1 \text{ O}_2 = 5$ mol.

Products (gases): $2 \text{ Cl}_2 + 2 \text{ H}_2\text{O} = 4$ mol.

So,

$ \Delta n = 4 - 5 = -1. $

Step 3: Work for 4 moles HCl

Since Δn = –1 for 4 moles of HCl:

$ w = -\Delta n \, R T = -(-1)(8.314)(300) = 2494.2 \,\text{J}. $

This corresponds to 4 moles of HCl reacting.

Step 4: Work per mole of HCl

For 1 mole of HCl:

$ w = \frac{2494.2}{4} = 623.55 \,\text{J}. $

Final Answer:

The work done for the oxidation of one mole of HCl is:

$ \boxed{623.6 \,\text{J}} $

Correct Option: B

content: Which from following is an example of an intensive property of the system?
explanation:
An intensive property is one that does not depend on the size or extent of the system, e.g., temperature, pressure, density, molar properties.

Let's analyze the options:

  • Option A: Volume → Extensive property (depends on amount of substance).

  • Option B: Enthalpy → Extensive property.

  • Option C: Entropy → Extensive property.

  • Option D: Molar volume (Volume per mole) → Intensive property, since it is independent of the system size.

✅ Correct Answer: Option D — Molar volume

content: For a certain reaction $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-225 \mathrm{~kJ}$ and $\Delta S=-150 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$. Find the temperature so that $\Delta G$ is zero.
explanation:
Temperature at which free energy change for the reaction will be zero ( $\Delta \mathrm{G}=0$ ) is,

$$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\Delta \mathrm{~S}}=\frac{-225000 \mathrm{~J}}{-150 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}}=1500 \mathrm{~K} $$

content: Calculate the work done if 1 mole of certain gas compressed isothermally and reversibly at 300 K from initial pressure $x$ bar to final pressure $2 x$ bar $\left[\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{W}_{\max } & =-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log _{10} \frac{\mathrm{P}_1}{\mathrm{P}_2} \\ & =-2.303 \times 1 \times 8.314 \times 300 \log _{10} \frac{x}{2 x} \\ & =-5744.14 \times \log _{10}(0.5) \\ & =-5744.14 \times(-0.3010) \\ & =1728.9 \mathrm{~J}=1.729 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned} $$

content:
Calculate the standard enthalpy change of following reaction.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CH}_{4(\mathrm{~s})}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)} \\ & \text { if } \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{-}\left(\mathrm{CH}_4\right)=-75 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^*\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right)=-390 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $$

$$ \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)=-286 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $$
explanation:
Step 1: Write the Reaction and Formula

The reaction is: $$ \mathrm{CH}_{4(\mathrm{~s})}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)} $$ We use this formula to find the enthalpy change: $$ \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} = \sum \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} (\text{Products}) - \sum \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} (\text{Reactants}) $$

Step 2: List the Given Enthalpies of Formation

$$ \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} (\mathrm{CH}_4) = -75~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1} $$ $$ \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{*} (\mathrm{CO}_2) = -390~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1} $$ $$ \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} (\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}) = -286~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1} $$ For elements like $ \mathrm{O}_2 $, $ \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} $ is always 0.

Step 3: Plug in the Values

First, add up the enthalpies for the products: $$ (-390) + 2 \times (-286) $$ Then, add up the enthalpies for the reactants: $$ (-75) + 0 \text{ (for } \mathrm{O}_2) $$ So, $$ \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} = [(-390) + 2(-286)] - [(-75) + 0] $$

Step 4: Do the Calculations

Calculate the numbers:
$2 \times (-286) = -572$
So the sum for products is: $$ -390 - 572 = -962 $$
For reactants: $-75$
Now, subtract: $$ -962 - (-75) = -962 + 75 = -887 $$

Step 5: State the Final Answer

The standard enthalpy change for the reaction is: $$ -887~\mathrm{kJ} $$

content: Identify from following the correct set of thermodynamic conditions for the reaction to be spontaneous at all temperature.
explanation: For exothermic reaction whare entropy increases When $\Delta H$ is negative $(\Delta H<0)$ and $\Delta S$ is positive ( $\Delta S>0$ ), then $\Delta G$ is negative regardless of temperature. Such reactions are spontaneous at all temperatures.

content: Calculate the change in internal energy of the system if work done by the system is 18 joule and absorbs heat 50 joule in a particular reaction.
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { According to the first law of thermodynamics, }\\ &\Delta U=Q+W=50 J+(-18 J)=+32 J \end{aligned} $$

content: Identify from following the correct set of thermodynamic conditions for a reaction to be nonspontaneous at all temperatures.
explanation:
$$ \Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S $$

If $\Delta H$ positive $(\Delta H>0)$ and $\Delta S$ is negative $(\Delta S<0)$ then $\Delta G$ is positive regardless of temperature. Such reactions are nonspontaneous at all temperatures

content: Calculate $\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {Iotal }}$ for a certain reaction if $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-150 \mathrm{~kJ}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}=32 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$ at 300 K .
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {surr }} & =-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{T}} \\ & =-\frac{(-150 \mathrm{~kJ})}{300 \mathrm{~K}} \\ & =+0.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}=+500 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \\ \Delta \mathrm{~S}_{\text {total }} & =\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {sys }}+\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {surr }} \\ & =+32 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}+500 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \\ & =+532 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$

content: Calculate the work done in joule if 1 mole of an ideal gas compressed from volume $24 \mathrm{dm}^3$ to $13 \mathrm{dm}^3$ at constant external pressure 3 bar.
explanation:
Step 1: Recall formula

Work of expansion/compression at constant external pressure:

$ w = -P_{\text{ext}} \Delta V $

Step 2: Volumes

Initial volume:

$ V_i = 24~\text{dm}^3 $

Final volume:

$ V_f = 13~\text{dm}^3 $

Change in volume:

$ \Delta V = V_f - V_i = 13 - 24 = -11~\text{dm}^3 $

Step 3: External pressure (convert units)

$ P_{\text{ext}} = 3~\text{bar} $

Recall:

$ 1~\text{bar·dm}^3 = 100~\text{J} $

Step 4: Work calculation

$ w = -P_{\text{ext}} \Delta V = - (3~\text{bar})(-11~\text{dm}^3) $

$ w = 33~\text{bar·dm}^3 $

Convert to joule:

$ w = 33 \times 100 = 3300~\text{J} $

✅ Final Answer:

The work done is 3300 J.

Correct Option: A (3300 J)

content: Which from following is an example of both intensive property and state function?
explanation:
• Internal energy (A):

  • It is a state function.

  • But it is extensive, not intensive.

    → ❌

  • Volume (B):

  • It is also a state function.

  • But extensive, not intensive.

    → ❌

  • Temperature (C):

  • It is an intensive property.

  • It is also a state function.

    → ✅

  • Entropy (D):

  • It is a state function.

  • But it is extensive.

    → ❌

✅ Correct Answer: Option C — Temperature

content: For a certain reaction, $\Delta \mathrm{H}=-210 \mathrm{~kJ}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}=-150 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ Find the temperature so that $\Delta \mathrm{G}=0$.
explanation:
We are given:

$ \Delta H = -210 \,\text{kJ}, \quad \Delta S = -150 \,\text{J K}^{-1} $

(Be careful with units; it seems the problem statement mistakenly wrote $\text{kJ K}^{-1}$ for entropy. Normally $\Delta S$ is in J/K.)

Step 1: Condition for $\Delta G = 0$

$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S $

At equilibrium ($\Delta G=0$):

$ \Delta H = T \Delta S $

$ T = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} $

Step 2: Substitute values

But first, make units consistent.

$\Delta H = -210 \,\text{kJ} = -210,000 \,\text{J}$

$\Delta S = -150 \,\text{J K}^{-1}$

$ T = \frac{-210,000}{-150} = 1400 \,\text{K} $

✅ Final Answer

The equilibrium temperature is:

Option C: 1400 K

content: Calculate $\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ for a reaction if $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}=120 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=28000 \mathrm{~J}$
explanation:
Step 1: Write the Formula

The formula that connects $\Delta \mathrm{G}^\circ$, $\Delta \mathrm{H}^\circ$, and $\Delta \mathrm{S}^\circ$ is: $$ \Delta \mathrm{G}^\circ = \Delta \mathrm{H}^\circ - T\Delta \mathrm{S}^\circ $$

Step 2: Substitute the Values

We are given $\Delta \mathrm{G}^\circ = 28000~\mathrm{J}$, $\Delta \mathrm{S}^\circ = 120~\mathrm{JK}^{-1}$, and the temperature $T = 298~\mathrm{K}$ (standard temperature).

Plugging in the values: $$ 28000~\mathrm{J} = \Delta \mathrm{H}^\circ - 298~\mathrm{K} \times 120~\mathrm{JK}^{-1} $$

Step 3: Calculate $T\Delta \mathrm{S}^\circ$

Multiply the temperature and entropy change: $$ 298~\mathrm{K} \times 120~\mathrm{JK}^{-1} = 35760~\mathrm{J} $$

Step 4: Rearrange and Solve for $\Delta \mathrm{H}^\circ$

Add $35760~\mathrm{J}$ to both sides to get: $$ \Delta \mathrm{H}^\circ = 28000~\mathrm{J} + 35760~\mathrm{J} $$

Add the numbers: $$ \Delta \mathrm{H}^\circ = 63760~\mathrm{J} $$

Step 5: Convert to Kilojoules

To convert joules to kilojoules, divide by 1000: $$ \Delta \mathrm{H}^\circ = 63.76~\mathrm{kJ} $$

content: Calculate the work done in joule if 1 mole of ideal gas is compressed from $25 \mathrm{dm}^3$ to $13 \mathrm{dm}^3$ at constant external pressure 4 bar.
explanation:
First, we are given:

$V_1 = 25~\mathrm{dm}^3$ (initial volume), $V_2 = 13~\mathrm{dm}^3$ (final volume), and the external pressure $P_\mathrm{ext} = 4~\mathrm{bar}$.

Work done by the gas when compressed at constant external pressure can be found using:

$W = -P_\mathrm{ext} \Delta V = -P_\mathrm{ext} (V_2 - V_1)$

Now, substitute the values:

$W = -4~\mathrm{bar} \times (13~\mathrm{dm}^3 - 25~\mathrm{dm}^3)$

$13 - 25 = -12$, so $W = -4~\mathrm{bar} \times (-12~\mathrm{dm}^3)$

Calculating this, $W = 48~\mathrm{dm}^3\,\mathrm{bar}$

Next, we need to convert $48~\mathrm{dm}^3\,\mathrm{bar}$ to joules. We know that $1~\mathrm{dm}^3\,\mathrm{bar} = 100~\mathrm{J}$.

So, $W = 48~\mathrm{dm}^3\,\mathrm{bar} \times 100~\mathrm{J}/(\mathrm{dm}^3\,\mathrm{bar}) = 4800~\mathrm{J}$

content: Which from following transformations is endothermic in nature?
explanation: Melting of ice. i.e. $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{s})} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{\ell})}$ transformation is endothermic in nature with $\Delta_{\text {fus }} \mathrm{H}=+6.01 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$.

content:
Calculate the standard enthalpy change for synthesis of ammonia gas from following data.

i. $\quad 2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{H}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}_1^0=95.4 \mathrm{~kJ}$

ii. $\quad \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{H}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}$;$$ \Delta_{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{H}_2^0=-187.6 \mathrm{~kJ} $$
explanation:
$$ \begin{gathered} 2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{H}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}, \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}_1^0=95.4 \mathrm{~kJ} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ \\ \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{H}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}, \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}_2^0=-187.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ \end{gathered} $$

Adding reactions (i) and (ii), we get,

$$ \begin{aligned} & 3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_3 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{H}=95.4 \mathrm{~kJ}+(-187.6 \mathrm{~kJ})=-92.2 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ for synthesis of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ gas (1 mole)

$$ =\frac{-92.2}{2}=-46.1 \mathrm{~kJ} $$

content: Identify from following the correct set of thermodynamic conditions for the reaction to be spontaneous below equilibrium temperature.
explanation:
We need to analyze spontaneity of a reaction based on Gibbs free energy:

$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S $

  • A reaction is spontaneous when $\Delta G < 0$.

  • Here, we are asked: "spontaneous below equilibrium temperature".

Step 1: General cases

  1. Case A: $\Delta H < 0, \Delta S > 0$

    Reaction is spontaneous at all temperatures (always $\Delta G < 0$).

  2. Case B: $\Delta H > 0, \Delta S < 0$

    Reaction is non-spontaneous at all temperatures ($\Delta G > 0$).

  3. Case C: $\Delta H > 0, \Delta S > 0$

    Reaction is spontaneous at high temperatures (since $T \Delta S$ dominates).

  4. Case D: $\Delta H < 0, \Delta S < 0$

    Reaction is spontaneous at low temperatures (since at low $T$, negative $\Delta H$ dominates, but at high $T$, $-T \Delta S$ becomes positive and can outweigh enthalpy).

Step 2: Identify the situation

  • Question: Spontaneous below equilibrium temperature → that corresponds to the case where spontaneity occurs only at low $T$.

That is exactly $\Delta H < 0, \Delta S < 0$.

✅ Correct Answer: Option A

$ \Delta H < 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta S < 0 $

content: Calculate work done if 1 mole of an ideal gas expands isothermally from $2 \mathrm{dm}^3$ to $2.8 \mathrm{dm}^3$ against constant external pressure 1 atm .
explanation:
Step 1: Recall work expression

Work done in irreversible expansion against a constant external pressure is

$ w = -P_\text{ext} \Delta V $

Step 2: Find change in volume

$ V_i = 2.0 \,\text{dm}^3, \quad V_f = 2.8 \,\text{dm}^3 $

$ \Delta V = V_f - V_i = 2.8 - 2.0 = 0.8 \,\text{dm}^3 $

Step 3: Convert into correct units

1 dm³ = 1 L.

So $\Delta V = 0.8 \,L$.

Work is usually in joules (SI units), and we know:

$ 1\,\text{L·atm} = 101.325 \, J $

So

$ w = -P_\text{ext} \Delta V = -(1\,\text{atm})(0.8 \,L) $

$ w = -0.8 \,\text{L·atm} $

$ w = -0.8 \times 101.325 \, J $

$ w = -81.06 \, J \, \approx -81.04 \, J $

✅ Final Answer:

Option B: $-81.04 \, \text{J}$

content: Which from following reactions is exothermic?
explanation:
$$ \mathrm{KOH}_{(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{HNO}_{3(\mathrm{aq})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{KNO}_{3(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$

This is a neutralization reaction of a strong base $(\mathrm{KOH})$ and a strong acid $\left(\mathrm{HNO}_3\right)$ giving salt $\left(\mathrm{KNO}_3\right)$ and water along with release of heal Thus, it is an exothermic reaction.

content: Calculate work done in isothermal reversible expansion of 1 mole ideal gas from initial pressure 10 bar to final pressure 1 bar at 300 K . $\left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$.
explanation:
Step 1: Write the formula for work in isothermal reversible expansion.

The formula is:
$$ W = -2.303\,nRT\,\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right) $$ where: $n$ = number of moles,
$R$ = universal gas constant,
$T$ = temperature in Kelvin,
$P_1$ = initial pressure,
$P_2$ = final pressure.

Step 2: Fill in the given values.

From the question, we have:
$n = 1$ mole,
$R = 8.314\,\mathrm{J}\,\mathrm{K}^{-1}\,\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$,
$T = 300~\mathrm{K}$,
$P_1 = 10~\mathrm{bar}$,
$P_2 = 1~\mathrm{bar}$.

Step 3: Substitute the numbers into the formula.

$$ W = -2.303 \times 1 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \log_{10} \left(\frac{10}{1}\right) $$

Step 4: Calculate the logarithm.

$\log_{10}(10/1) = \log_{10}(10) = 1$.

Step 5: Multiply the values.

$W = -2.303 \times 1 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 1$

$W = -5744~\mathrm{J}$

$W = -5.74~\mathrm{kJ}$ (Dividing by 1000 to convert J to kJ)

content:
Calculate the standard enthalpy change for reaction,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}_{(l)}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(l)} \\ & \text { if } \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}\right)=-280 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right)=-390 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)=-285 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$
explanation:
Step 1: Write the reaction and important values

The reaction is: $$\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{OH}_{(l)}+3\ \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \longrightarrow 2\ \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}+3\ \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(l)}$$

We are given these standard enthalpy values (in kJ mol−1):

$$\Delta_\mathrm{f}H^\circ(\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{OH}) = -280$$

$$\Delta_\mathrm{f}H^\circ(\mathrm{CO}_2) = -390$$

$$\Delta_\mathrm{f}H^\circ(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) = -285$$

The enthalpy of formation for $$\mathrm{O}_2$$ is 0, since it is an element in its standard state.

Step 2: Write the formula for enthalpy change

The standard enthalpy change of the reaction ($$\Delta H^\circ$$) is calculated like this:

$$ \Delta H^\circ = [\text{Sum of enthalpies of products}] - [\text{Sum of enthalpies of reactants}] $$

For this reaction: $$ \Delta H^\circ = [2 \Delta_\mathrm{f} H^\circ(\mathrm{CO}_2) + 3 \Delta_\mathrm{f} H^\circ(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O})] - [\Delta_\mathrm{f} H^\circ(\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{OH}) + 3 \Delta_\mathrm{f} H^\circ(\mathrm{O}_2)] $$

Step 3: Substitute values and calculate

Now put in the numbers:

$$ = [2 \times (-390) + 3 \times (-285)] - [(-280) + 3 \times 0] $$

Calculate inside the brackets:

$$ = [(-780) + (-855)] - (-280) $$

Now add and subtract:

$$ = -1635 - (-280) $$

Subtracting a negative is the same as adding a positive:

$$ = -1635 + 280 = -1355~\mathrm{kJ} $$

content: Identify from following reactions that exhibits negative work done.
explanation:
$$ \mathrm{W}=-\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{RT} $$

For the reaction, $2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{2(l)} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(l)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$

$$ \Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=1-0=1 $$

Work $=-1 \mathrm{RT}=-\mathrm{ve}$

content: Calculate the enthalpy of solution of potassium chloride if its $\Delta_{\mathrm{L}} \mathrm{H}=700 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $\Delta_{\text {hyd }} H=-680 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
explanation:
$ \Delta_\mathrm{L} H = 700 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} \quad \text{(lattice enthalpy)} $

$ \Delta_\mathrm{hyd} H = -680 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} \quad \text{(enthalpy of hydration)} $

Step 1: Relation between terms

The enthalpy of solution is:

$ \Delta_\mathrm{sol} H = \Delta_\mathrm{L} H + \Delta_\mathrm{hyd} H $

  • Lattice enthalpy = energy required to break the ionic lattice into gaseous ions (endothermic, positive).

  • Hydration enthalpy = energy released when gaseous ions are solvated by water (exothermic, negative).

Step 2: Substitute values

$ \Delta_\mathrm{sol} H = (+700) + (-680) $

$ \Delta_\mathrm{sol} H = +20 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $

Step 3: Pick correct answer

$ \boxed{20 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}} $

Correct Option: A

content: Calculate the change in internal energy of the system if 20 kJ work is done on the system and it releases 10 kJ heat in a particular reaction.
explanation:
We apply the first law of thermodynamics:

$ \Delta U = Q + W $

where

  • $\Delta U$ = change in internal energy

  • $Q$ = heat absorbed by the system

  • $W$ = work done on the system

Given data:

  • Work done on the system = $+20 \, \text{kJ}$

  • System releases $10 \, \text{kJ}$ of heat → $Q = -10 \, \text{kJ}$

Calculation:

$ \Delta U = Q + W = (-10) + (20) = +10 \, \text{kJ} $

✅ Answer: Option C: 10 kJ

content: Calculate the work done in joule if 2 moles of an ideal gas expand isothermally from $15.5 \mathrm{dm}^3$ to $20 \mathrm{dm}^3$ at constant pressure 1 atm .
explanation:
Step 1: Recall the work expression

For expansion work against external constant pressure:

$ w = -P_{\text{ext}} \, \Delta V $

(Sign convention: in chemistry, work done by the system is taken as negative.)

Step 2: Extract quantities

Initial volume:

$ V_1 = 15.5 \,\text{dm}^3 = 15.5 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^3 $

Final volume:

$ V_2 = 20 \,\text{dm}^3 = 20 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^3 $

Change in volume:

$ \Delta V = (20-15.5)\,\text{dm}^3 = 4.5 \,\text{dm}^3 $

Convert to liters/m³:

Since $1 \,\text{dm}^3 = 1 \, L$: $\Delta V = 4.5 \, L$.

Also, $1 \, L = 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$.

So, $\Delta V = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^3$.

Step 3: Pressure in SI units

$ 1 \,\text{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} $

Step 4: Work Calculation

$ w = -P \Delta V = -(1.013 \times 10^5)(4.5 \times 10^{-3}) $

$ w = -(1.013 \times 10^5 \times 0.0045) $

$ = -(456) \,\text{J} $

Step 5: Match with options

The closest option is: A: -456 J

✅ Final Answer: Option A (-456 J)

content: 1.8 g water is vapourised by supplying 4 kJ heat at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the heat of vapourisation of water at same temperature?
explanation:
Step 1: Data given

  • Mass of water vaporized, $m = 1.8 \ \text{g}$

  • Heat absorbed, $q = 4 \ \text{kJ}$

  • Molar mass of water, $M = 18 \ \text{g mol}^{-1}$

Step 2: Moles of water vaporized

$ n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{1.8}{18} = 0.1 \ \text{mol} $

Step 3: Heat supplied per mole (molar enthalpy of vaporization)

$ \Delta H_{vap} = \frac{q}{n} = \frac{4 \ \text{kJ}}{0.1 \ \text{mol}} $

$ \Delta H_{vap} = 40 \ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} $

✅ Final Answer:

The heat of vaporization is

Option B: $40 \ \mathrm{kJ \ mol^{-1}}$

content:
Calculate the standard enthalpy change of following reaction

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CH}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)} \\ & \text { If } \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_4\right)=-75 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right)=-394 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)=-286 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$
explanation:
$$ \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=\sum \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}{ }_{(\text {products })}-\sum \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}{ }_{(\text {reactants })} $$

$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}{ }_{\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)}=0$, because it is in its standard elemental state.

$$ \begin{aligned} \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} & =\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right)}^{\circ}+2 \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}_{\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)}^{\circ}-\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_4\right) \\ & =-394+2(-286)-(-75) \\ & =-394-572+75 \\ & =-891 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned} $$

content:
Calculate standard enthalpy change of reaction

$$ \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})}, \text { if } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right)=-393 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)=-286 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_2\right)=227 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$
explanation:
We are asked to calculate the standard enthalpy change of reaction:

$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{5}{2} \text{O}_2(g) \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; 2 \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2O(l) $

Given data:

$ \Delta_f H^\circ(\text{CO}_2) = -393 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}, \quad \Delta_f H^\circ(\text{H}_2O) = -286 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}, \quad \Delta_f H^\circ(\text{C}_2H_2) = +227 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $

Step 1: General formula

Standard enthalpy change of reaction ($\Delta_r H^\circ$) is:

$ \Delta_r H^\circ = \sum \Delta_f H^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta_f H^\circ(\text{reactants}) $

Step 2: Products

  • For 2 mol CO$_2$: $2 \times (-393) = -786 \,\text{kJ}$

  • For 1 mol H$_2$O(l): $1 \times (-286) = -286 \,\text{kJ}$

$ \sum \Delta_f H^\circ(\text{products}) = -786 + (-286) = -1072 \,\text{kJ} $

Step 3: Reactants

  • For 1 mol C$_2$H$_2$: $+227 \,\text{kJ}$

  • For O$_2$: standard enthalpy of formation = 0

$ \sum \Delta_f H^\circ(\text{reactants}) = 227 \,\text{kJ} $

Step 4: Reaction enthalpy

$ \Delta_r H^\circ = (-1072) - (227) = -1299 \,\text{kJ} $

✅ Final Answer:

$ \boxed{-1299 \,\text{kJ}} $

Correct option: C

content: Calculate the change in internal energy of the system if 25 kJ of work done by the system and it absorbs 10 kJ of heat.
explanation:
We use the First Law of Thermodynamics:

$ \Delta U = Q - W $

Where:

  • $\Delta U$ = change in internal energy

  • $Q$ = heat absorbed by the system (positive if absorbed)

  • $W$ = work done by the system (positive if system does work)

Given:

  • $Q = +10 \, \text{kJ}$ (absorbed)

  • $W = +25 \, \text{kJ}$ (done by system)

$ \Delta U = 10 - 25 = -15 \, \text{kJ} $

✅ Correct Answer: Option C: -15 kJ

content: Identify from following an example of intensive property?
explanation:
An intensive property is a property that does not depend on the amount of matter present (e.g., temperature, pressure, density, surface tension).

An extensive property depends on the amount of matter (e.g., volume, number of moles, internal energy).

  • Surface tension → Intensive ✅

  • Volume → Extensive

  • Internal energy → Extensive

  • Number of moles → Extensive

✅ Correct Answer: Option A: Surface tension

content:
Calculate the work done in following reaction at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4 \mathrm{SO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{SO}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} \\ & \left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \end{aligned} $$
explanation:
Given reaction:

$$ 4 \mathrm{SO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} + 2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{SO}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} $$

Step 1: Calculate $\Delta n_{\text{g}}$ (change in moles of gas):

  • Reactant gas moles: $4$ (SO$_2$) $+$ $2$ (O$_2$) $=$ $6$

  • Product gas moles: $4$ (SO$_3$)

Therefore,

$$ \Delta n_{\text{g}} = \text{Product gas moles} - \text{Reactant gas moles} = 4 - 6 = -2 $$

Step 2: Write the formula for work done $(W)$ at constant temperature and pressure:

At constant pressure,

$$ W = -P \Delta V $$

For gaseous reactions at constant $T$ and $P$,

$$ \Delta V = \Delta n_{\text{g}} \cdot R T / P $$

So,

$$ W = -\Delta n_{\text{g}} ~R~ T $$

Step 3: Substitute the values:

  • $\Delta n_{\text{g}} = -2$

  • $R = 8.314$ J K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1}$

  • $T = 27^\circ$C $= 27 + 273 = 300$ K

$$ W = -(-2) \times 8.314 \times 300 $$

$$ W = 2 \times 8.314 \times 300 $$

$$ W = 2 \times 2494.2 = 4988.4~\text{J} $$

Final Answer:

$\boxed{4988.4~\text{J}}$

So, the correct option is A) 4988.4 J.

content: Which of the following set of thermodynamic conditions is true for the reaction to be spontaneous at high temperature?
explanation:
To determine the spontaneity of a reaction, we use the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$):

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S $$

A reaction is spontaneous if $\Delta G < 0$.

Let us analyse each option for high temperature ($T$ is large):

Option A: $\Delta H < 0$ and $\Delta S < 0$

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S $$

Here,

  • $\Delta H$ is negative (favourable)

  • $\Delta S$ is negative, so $-T\Delta S$ becomes positive and increases with $T$ (unfavourable at high $T$)

  • At high $T$, $\Delta G$ can become positive $\rightarrow$ Not always spontaneous at high $T$

Option B: $\Delta H > 0$ and $\Delta S > 0$

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S $$

Here,

  • $\Delta H$ is positive (unfavourable)

  • $\Delta S$ is positive, so $-T\Delta S$ is negative (favourable), and at high $T$, this term dominates

  • At very high $T$, $-T\Delta S$ can outweigh $\Delta H$, making $\Delta G < 0$

  • Spontaneous at high $T$

Option C: $\Delta H < 0$ and $\Delta S > 0$

  • Both terms are favourable

  • Reaction will be spontaneous at all temperatures, not just at high $T$

Option D: $\Delta H > 0$ and $\Delta S < 0$

  • Both terms are unfavourable

  • Reaction is non-spontaneous at any temperature

Correct Answer:

Option B

$\Delta H > 0$ and $\Delta S > 0$

content:
Find standard enthalpy of formation of ammonia from following reaction.

$$ \mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^0=-92.0 \mathrm{~kJ} $$
explanation:
Given reaction:

$$ \mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} H^0 = -92.0~\mathrm{kJ} $$

Step 1: Definition

The standard enthalpy of formation ($\Delta_\mathrm{f} H^0$) is the enthalpy change when 1 mole of a compound is formed from its elements in their standard states.

Step 2: According to the reaction

The above reaction forms 2 moles of ammonia ($\mathrm{NH}_3$) and the enthalpy change is for 2 moles: $-92.0~\mathrm{kJ}$.

Step 3: Find enthalpy for 1 mole

$\therefore$ For 1 mole of ammonia:

$$ \Delta_\mathrm{f} H^0 = \frac{-92.0~\mathrm{kJ}}{2} = -46.0~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1} $$

Step 4: Final Answer

So, the standard enthalpy of formation of ammonia is

$$ \boxed{-46.0~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1}} $$

Correct option: C

content: Calculate work done if 1 mole of an ideal gas compressed isothermally and reversibly from 12 $\mathrm{dm}^3$ to $6 \mathrm{dm}^3$ at $300 \mathrm{~K} .\left[\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
explanation:
Given:

  • Number of moles, $n = 1$

  • Initial volume, $V_1 = 12~\text{dm}^3 = 12 \times 10^{-3}~\text{m}^3$

  • Final volume, $V_2 = 6~\text{dm}^3 = 6 \times 10^{-3}~\text{m}^3$

  • Temperature, $T = 300~\text{K}$

  • Gas constant, $R = 8.314~\text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$

For an isothermal, reversible compression of an ideal gas,

the work done ($w$) by the gas is given by:

$$ w = -nRT \ln \left( \frac{V_2}{V_1} \right) $$

Step 1: Plug in the values:

$$ w = -1 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln\left( \frac{6}{12} \right) $$

Step 2: Simplify $\frac{6}{12}$:

$$ \frac{6}{12} = 0.5 $$

Step 3: Find $\ln(0.5)$:

$$ \ln(0.5) = -0.6931 $$

Step 4: Substitute and solve:

$$ w = -1 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times (-0.6931) $$

$$ w = 8.314 \times 300 \times 0.6931 $$

Step 5: Multiply:

First, calculate $8.314 \times 300 = 2494.2$

Now multiply by $0.6931$:

$$ w = 2494.2 \times 0.6931 = 1728.88~\text{J} $$

Step 6: Convert J to kJ:

$$ w = \frac{1728.88}{1000} = 1.729~\text{kJ} $$

Final Answer:

The work done is $1.729~\text{kJ}$.

Correct option: A

content: Identify from following physical transformations that exhibits decrease in entropy.
explanation:
Entropy is a measure of disorder or randomness in a system. According to NCERT, when a substance changes its state from solid to liquid to gas, entropy increases because the randomness increases. Conversely, when a substance changes state from gas to liquid to solid, the entropy decreases.

Now let's look at each option:

Option A:

$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(l)}$

(Solid to liquid ⇒ entropy increases.)

Option B:

$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(l)} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(g)}$

(Liquid to gas ⇒ entropy increases.)

Option C:

$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(g)}$

(Solid to gas ⇒ entropy increases.)

Option D:

$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(g)} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(l)}$

(Gas to liquid ⇒ entropy decreases.)

Correct Answer:

$\boxed{\text{Option D}}$

$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(g)} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(l)}$ shows a decrease in entropy.

content:
Calculate the standard enthalpy change of following reaction.

$$ \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(l)} $$

If $\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4\right)=-52 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right)=-390 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)=-286 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$
explanation:
Given reaction:

$$ \mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_{4(g)} + 3\ \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \longrightarrow 2\ \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)} + 2\ \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(l)} $$

Given data:

$ \Delta_\mathrm{f}H^\circ(\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_4) = -52~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1} $

$ \Delta_\mathrm{f}H^\circ(\mathrm{CO}_2) = -390~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1} $

$ \Delta_\mathrm{f}H^\circ(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) = -286~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1} $

Step 1: Write the general formula (using NCERT convention):

$ \Delta_\mathrm{r}H^\circ = \sum \text{Enthalpy of formation of products} - \sum \text{Enthalpy of formation of reactants} $

Step 2: Substitute the values for the reaction:

  • Products: $2 \mathrm{CO}_2$ and $2 \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$

  • Reactants: $1~\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_4$ and $3~\mathrm{O}_2$

Note: Standard enthalpy of formation for elements in their standard states (like $\mathrm{O}_2$ gas) is zero.

$ \Delta_\mathrm{r}H^\circ = [2 \times \Delta_\mathrm{f} H^\circ (\mathrm{CO}_2) + 2 \times \Delta_\mathrm{f} H^\circ (\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O})] - [1\times \Delta_\mathrm{f} H^\circ (\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_4) + 3\times \Delta_\mathrm{f} H^\circ (\mathrm{O}_2)] $

$ \Delta_\mathrm{r}H^\circ = [2 \times (-390) + 2 \times (-286)] - [1 \times (-52) + 3 \times (0)] $

$ = [(-780) + (-572)] - [-52] $

$ = (-1352) - (-52) $

$ = -1352 + 52 $

$ = -1300~\mathrm{kJ} $

Final Answer:

The standard enthalpy change for the given reaction is:

$ \boxed{-1300~\mathrm{kJ}} $

(Option B)

content: Which from following process involves zero work done?
explanation:
The correct answer is:

Option D: Free expansion of gas.

Explanation:

  • Work done by a gas is given by:

    $$ W = \int_{V_i}^{V_f} P \, dV $$

    where $P$ is the external pressure.

  • In free expansion, the gas expands into a vacuum. That is, external pressure, $P = 0$.

  • Therefore,

    $$ W = \int_{V_i}^{V_f} 0 \, dV = 0 $$

So, work done in free expansion of gas is zero.

Other options involve non-zero work done because:

  • Isobaric process: Pressure is constant, gas does work as volume changes.

  • Adiabatic process: No heat exchange, but work can be done by/on the gas.

  • Isothermal process: Temperature constant, work is done during volume change.

Final Answer:

Option D: Free expansion of gas.

content: Calculate the constant external pressure required to expand 2 moles of an ideal gas from volume $15 \mathrm{dm}^3$ to $20 \mathrm{dm}^3$ if amount of work done is -600 J .
explanation:
Given:

  • Initial volume, $V_1 = 15 \ \mathrm{dm}^3$

  • Final volume, $V_2 = 20 \ \mathrm{dm}^3$

  • Amount of work done, $w = -600 \ \mathrm{J}$

  • Number of moles, $n = 2$

Step 1: Write the formula for work done at constant pressure, $P_{ext}$:

For expansion at constant external pressure:

$$ w = -P_{ext} \Delta V $$

Where $\Delta V = V_2 - V_1$.

Step 2: Calculate $\Delta V$:

$$ \Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = 20 \ \mathrm{dm}^3 - 15 \ \mathrm{dm}^3 = 5 \ \mathrm{dm}^3 $$

Convert $\mathrm{dm}^3$ to $\mathrm{m}^3$:

$$ 1 \ \mathrm{dm}^3 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{m}^3 $$

So,

$$ \Delta V = 5 \ \mathrm{dm}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{m}^3 $$

Step 3: Substitute values into the work formula and solve for $P_{ext}$:

$$ w = -P_{ext} \Delta V $$

Given $w = -600 \ \mathrm{J}$, so:

$$ -600 = -P_{ext} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} $$

Divide both sides by $-1$:

$$ 600 = P_{ext} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ P_{ext} = \frac{600}{5 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ P_{ext} = \frac{600}{0.005} $$

$$ P_{ext} = 120000 \ \mathrm{Pa} $$

Step 4: Convert $\mathrm{Pa}$ to $\mathrm{bar}$:

$$ 1 \ \mathrm{bar} = 10^5 \ \mathrm{Pa} $$

So,

$$ P_{ext} = \frac{120000}{100000} = 1.2 \ \mathrm{bar} $$

Final Answer:

Option A: $1.2 \ \mathrm{bar}$

content: Calculate the enthalpy of vaporisation of ethanol if 11.5 g of ethanol is completely vaporised by supplying 11.8 kJ of heat.
explanation:
Given:

  • Mass of ethanol vapourised, $m = 11.5~\mathrm{g}$

  • Heat supplied, $q = 11.8~\mathrm{kJ}$

  • Molar mass of ethanol $(C_2H_5OH)$, $M = 46~\mathrm{g~mol}^{-1}$

Step 1: Calculate moles of ethanol vaporised

$ \text{Moles of ethanol},~ n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{11.5~\mathrm{g}}{46~\mathrm{g~mol}^{-1}} = 0.25~\mathrm{mol} $

Step 2: Use the formula for enthalpy of vaporisation per mole

Enthalpy of vaporisation, $\Delta H_\text{vap}$, is the heat required to vaporise 1 mol.

$ \Delta H_\text{vap} = \frac{q}{n} $

$ \Delta H_\text{vap} = \frac{11.8~\mathrm{kJ}}{0.25~\mathrm{mol}} = 47.2~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1} $

Answer:

Option B $47.2~\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1}$

content: Calculate the enthalpy change of vaporisation of benzene if 13 gram of benzene vaporised by supplying 5.1 kJ of heat.
explanation:
Given:

  • Mass of benzene vaporised, $m = 13\,\mathrm{g}$

  • Heat supplied, $q = 5.1\,\mathrm{kJ}$

  • Molar mass of benzene, $M = 78\,\mathrm{g~mol}^{-1}$

We have to find enthalpy change of vaporisation of benzene ($\Delta_\mathrm{vap} H$), which is the heat required to vaporise 1 mole of benzene.

Step 1: Calculate moles of benzene vaporised.

$ \text{Moles of benzene} = \frac{\text{given mass}}{\text{molar mass}} = \frac{13}{78} = 0.1667 $

Step 2: Calculate enthalpy change of vaporisation for 1 mole.

Let the molar enthalpy of vaporisation be $x$ kJ/mol.

$ 0.1667\;\text{mol} \text{ requires } 5.1\;\mathrm{kJ} $

So,

$ 1\;\text{mol} \text{ requires } \frac{5.1}{0.1667}\;\mathrm{kJ} $

$ x = \frac{5.1}{0.1667} $

$ x \approx 30.6\;\mathrm{kJ~mol}^{-1} $

Final Answer:

Option C: $30.6\;\mathrm{kJ}~\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$

content: Calculate the entropy change of surrounding if 2 moles of $\mathrm{H}_2$ and 1 mole of $\mathrm{O}_2$ gas combine to form 2 moles of liquid water by releasing 525 kJ heat to surrounding at constant pressure and at 300 K .
explanation:
Given:

  • $q = 525~\text{kJ}$ heat is released (exothermic process, so $q = -525~\text{kJ}$)

  • $T = 300~\text{K}$

  • Pressure is constant.

Step 1: Entropy change of surroundings ($\Delta S_{\text{surroundings}}$) is calculated by:

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} = \frac{q_{\text{surroundings}}}{T} $$

Since heat is released by the system, the same amount of heat is gained by the surroundings. So,

$$ q_{\text{surroundings}} = +525~\text{kJ} = 525,000~\text{J} $$

Step 2: Substitute the values:

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} = \frac{525,000~\text{J}}{300~\text{K}} $$

Step 3: Calculate the value:

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} = \frac{525,000}{300} = 1750~\text{J}~\text{K}^{-1} $$

Step 4: Choose the correct option:

So, the correct answer is:

$ \boxed{1750~\mathrm{J}~\mathrm{K}^{-1}} $

Option B is correct.

content: Which of the following reactions exhibits decrease in entropy?
explanation:
Let us carefully check each option.

Entropy is a measure of disorder or randomness. General rules:

  • Gases have highest entropy, then liquids, then solids.

  • If number of gas molecules increases → entropy increases.

  • If gas molecules decrease or gases convert into liquid/solid → entropy decreases.

Option A

$ 2 \, H_2O_{2(l)} \longrightarrow 2 \, H_2O_{(l)} + O_{2(g)} $

Here, a gas ($O_2$) is produced whereas reactant is only liquid.

→ Increase in entropy.

Option B

$ H_{2(g)} \longrightarrow 2 \, H_{(g)} $

Molecules break into atoms, both are in gaseous form. Disorder increases.

→ Increase in entropy.

Option C

$ CaCO_{3(s)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} CaO_{(s)} + CO_{2(g)} $

A solid gives solid + gas. Gas formed → entropy increases.

→ Increase in entropy.

Option D

$ 2 H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)} \longrightarrow 2 H_2O_{(l)} $

Reactants: 3 moles of gas.

Products: only liquid (no gas).

→ Gas molecules disappear, disorder decreases.

→ Entropy decreases.

Correct Answer: Option D

content: Calculate $\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {total }}$ for a certain reaction at 298 K if $\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-208.6 \mathrm{~kJ}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}=-36 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$
explanation:
Given:

  • $\Delta H^{\circ} = -208.6\ \text{kJ}$

  • $\Delta S^{\circ} = -36\ \text{J}\ \text{K}^{-1}$

  • $T = 298\,\text{K}$

First, convert $\Delta H^{\circ}$ from kJ to J:

$$ \Delta H^{\circ} = -208.6\ \text{kJ} = -208600\ \text{J} $$

Step 1: Calculate the entropy change of the surroundings ($\Delta S_{\text{surroundings}}$):

By NCERT method,

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T} $$

Substitute the values:

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} = -\frac{-208600\ \text{J}}{298\ \text{K}} = \frac{208600}{298}\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

Calculate:

$$ \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} \approx 700\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

Step 2: Calculate the total entropy change ($\Delta S_{\text{total}}$):

$$ \Delta S_{\text{total}} = \Delta S^{\circ}_{\text{system}} + \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} $$

Substitute the values:

$$ \Delta S_{\text{total}} = -36\ \text{J K}^{-1} + 700\ \text{J K}^{-1} = 664\ \text{J K}^{-1} $$

Final Answer:

Option A: $664\ \text{J K}^{-1}$

content: Calculate the change in internal energy of the system if 20 kJ of work is done on the system and it releases 10 kJ of heat in a particular reaction.
explanation:
According to the First Law of Thermodynamics, the change in internal energy $(\Delta U)$ is given by:

$$ \Delta U = q + w $$

Where:

  • $q$ is the heat absorbed by the system.

  • $w$ is the work done on the system.

Given:

  • Work done on the system, $w = +20\,\text{kJ}$

  • System releases heat, so $q = -10\,\text{kJ}$ (since heat is released by the system)

Substitute the values:

$$ \Delta U = (-10) + (20) $$

$$ \Delta U = 10\,\text{kJ} $$

Final Answer:

Option B: 10 kJ

content: Which from following reactions performs zero work?
explanation:
To determine which reaction performs zero work, we need to recall that work done by a reaction at constant pressure is:

$$ w = -P \Delta V $$

For gases, change in volume is proportional to the change in number of moles of gases ($\Delta n_{gas}$):

$$ \Delta V \propto \Delta n_{gas} $$

If $\Delta n_{gas} = 0$, then $w = 0$.

Let's calculate $\Delta n_{gas}$ for each option:

Option A

$\mathrm{CH}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} + \mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})} + \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})}$

Moles of gaseous reactants $= 1 + 1 = 2$

Moles of gaseous products $= 1 + 1 = 2$

$\Delta n_{gas} = 2 - 2 = 0$

Option B

$3 \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}) + \mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2\mathrm{NH}_3(\mathrm{~g}) $

Moles of gaseous reactants $= 3 + 1 = 4$

Moles of gaseous products $= 2$

$\Delta n_{gas} = 2 - 4 = -2$

Option C

$\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}) + \frac{5}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) + \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(l)}$

Moles of gaseous reactants $= 1 + 2.5 = 3.5$

Moles of gaseous products $= 2$

$\Delta n_{gas} = 2 - 3.5 = -1.5$

Option D

$2\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_6(\mathrm{~g}) + 7\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 4\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) + 6\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_{(l)}$

Moles of gaseous reactants $= 2 + 7 = 9$

Moles of gaseous products $= 4$

$\Delta n_{gas} = 4 - 9 = -5$

Conclusion:

Option A performs zero work because the number of moles of gaseous reactants equals the number of moles of gaseous products.

Final answer:

$ \boxed{\text{Option A}} $

content: Calculate standard internal energy change for $\mathrm{OF}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HF}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ at 300 K , if $\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ of $\mathrm{OF}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ and $\mathrm{HF}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ are 20, -250 and $-270 \quad \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. $\left[\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=\sum \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} \text { of product }-\sum \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ} \text { of Reactants } \\ & =[(2 \times-270+0)-(20-250)] \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & =-310 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Delta H^{\circ}=\Delta U^{\circ}+\Delta n_g R T \\ & \Delta \mathrm{U}^{\circ}=\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}-\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{RT} \\ & =-310 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}-(3-2) \times \\ & 8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{U}^{\circ}=-307.50 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Calculate internal energy change of a system if work done by the system is 8 J and heat supplied to it is 40 J .
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \Delta U=q+w \\ & \Delta U=40 J+(-8 J)=32 J \end{aligned}$$

content: Which of the following equation relates temperature of a reaction with $\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}$ at equilibrium?
explanation:
At equilibrium, the relationship between the temperature of a reaction, the standard change in enthalpy ($\Delta H^{\circ}$), and the standard change in entropy ($\Delta S^{\circ}$) is described by the Gibbs free energy equation. At equilibrium, the change in Gibbs free energy ($\Delta G^{\circ}$) is zero, so:

$$ \Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ} = 0 $$

Rearranging the equation gives:

$$ \Delta H^{\circ} = T \Delta S^{\circ} $$

Therefore, the correct option is Option C:

$$ \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ} = \mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ} $$

This equation indicates that at equilibrium, the enthalpy changes are directly proportional to the temperature and entropy changes of the reaction.

content: The enthalpy of vaporisation of a liquid is $30 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and entropy of vaporisation is $75 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Calculate boiling point of liquid at 1 atm .
explanation:
The boiling point of a liquid at 1 atm can be calculated using the Gibbs free energy change for the phase transition. At the boiling point, the change in Gibbs free energy ($\Delta G$) is zero for the vaporization process. The relationship between enthalpy, entropy, and temperature at equilibrium is given by:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S = 0 $$

Therefore,

$$ \Delta H = T \Delta S $$

where:

$\Delta H = 30 \, \mathrm{kJ} \, \mathrm{mol}^{-1} = 30000 \, \mathrm{J} \, \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ (since $1 \, \mathrm{kJ} = 1000 \, \mathrm{J}$)

$\Delta S = 75 \, \mathrm{J} \, \mathrm{K}^{-1} \, \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$

Rearrange the equation to solve for the boiling point temperature $T$:

$$ T = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} = \frac{30000 \, \mathrm{J} \, \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{75 \, \mathrm{J} \, \mathrm{K}^{-1} \, \mathrm{mol}^{-1}} $$

Calculating this gives:

$$ T = 400 \, \mathrm{K} $$

Thus, the boiling point of the liquid at 1 atm is 400 K.

Option B: 400 K is correct.

content: Find work done on 2 mole of an ideal gas at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ if it is compressed reversibly and isothermally from $5.05 \times 10^6 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ to $1.01 \times 10^5 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ pressure.
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{W}_{\max } & =-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log _{10}\left(\frac{\mathrm{~V}_2}{\mathrm{~V}_1}\right) \\ & =-2.303 \times 2 \mathrm{~mol} \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \log _{10} \frac{1.01 \times 10^5}{5.05 \times 10^6} \\ & =19.52 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned}$$

content: A system performs mechanical work equal to 15 kJ and looses 2 kJ of heat to surrounding. What is the change in internal energy of a system?
explanation:
$$\mathrm{W}=-15 \mathrm{~kJ}, \mathrm{Q}=-2 \mathrm{~kJ}$$

According to the first law of thermodynamics,

$$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}=-2+(-15)=-17 \mathrm{~kJ}$$

content: Find the constant external pressure required to expand a gas from 2.5 L to 4.5 L if amount of work done is 500 J at 298 K ?
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{V}_1 & =2.5 \mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{~V}_2=4.5 \mathrm{~L}, \\ \mathrm{~W} & =-500 \mathrm{~J}=-5 \mathrm{dm}^3 \text { bar }\left(\because 100 \mathrm{~J}=1 \mathrm{dm}^3 \text { bar }\right) \\ \mathrm{W} & =-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \Delta \mathrm{V} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}} & =-\frac{\mathrm{W}}{\Delta \mathrm{~V}}=-\frac{\mathrm{W}}{\left(\mathrm{~V}_2-\mathrm{V}_1\right)} \\ & =\frac{-\left(-5 \mathrm{dm}^3 \text { bar }\right)}{(4.5-2.5) \mathrm{dm}^3}=2.5 \mathrm{bar} \end{aligned}$$

content: In a particular reaction ' x ' kJ of heat is released by the system and ' y ' kJ of work done is done on the system. What is internal energy change?
explanation:
$$\mathrm{Q}=-\mathrm{xkJ}, \mathrm{~W}=+\mathrm{ykJ}$$

According to the first law of thermodynamics,

$$\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{U} =\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W} \\ \therefore \quad & \Delta \mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{ykJ}=\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{xkJ} \end{aligned}$$

content: Calculate the value of $\Delta G$ for the following reaction. $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_{4(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ if $\Delta \mathrm{H}=57.44 \mathrm{~kJ}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}=176 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{H} & =57.44 \mathrm{~kJ} \\ \Delta \mathrm{~S} & =176 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}=176 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \\ \mathrm{~T} & =300 \mathrm{~K} \\ \Delta \mathrm{G} & =\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{~S} \\ \therefore \quad \Delta \mathrm{G} & =57.44 \mathrm{~kJ}-\left(300 \mathrm{~K} \times 176 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\right) \\ & =57.44 \mathrm{~kJ}-52.8 \mathrm{~kJ}=4.64 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned}$$

content: Under similar conditions enthalpy of freezing is exactly opposite to
explanation:
For the reaction,

Under similar conditions of $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atm pressure, $\Delta_{\mathrm{fus}} \mathrm{H}$ is $+6.01 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, whereas $\Delta_{\text {freez }} \mathrm{H}$ is $-6.01 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Thus, enthalpy of freezing is exactly opposite to enthalpy of fusion.

content:
100 ml of $\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ and 100 ml of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ were allowed to react at 1 bar pressure as

$$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$

What will be the PV type of work done during reaction?
explanation:
1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_2$ reacts with 1 mole of $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ to produce 2 moles of HCl.

Hence, 100 mL of $\mathrm{H}_2$ would react with 100 mL of $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ to produce 200 mL of HCl .

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { of } \mathrm{Cl}_2 \text { to produce 200 mL of HCl. } \\ & \mathrm{V}_1=100+100=200 \mathrm{~mL}=0.2 \mathrm{dm}^3 \\ & \mathrm{~V}_2=200 \mathrm{~mL}=0.2 \mathrm{dm}^3 \\ & \mathrm{~W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \Delta \mathrm{V}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}\left(\mathrm{V}_2-\mathrm{V}_1\right) \\ & \mathrm{W}=-1(0.2-0.2) \end{aligned}$$

$$=\text { zero } \mathrm{J}\quad$$ $\left(\because 100 \mathrm{~J}=1 \mathrm{dm}^3 \mathrm{bar}\right)$

content: Identify the process from following such that volume of system remains constant.
explanation:
The process in which the volume of the system remains constant is the isochoric process.

In an isochoric process, the volume does not change, which means:

$$ \Delta V = 0 $$

Since the volume remains constant, the work done by the system is zero, because work is given by the formula:

$$ W = P \Delta V $$

Here, $ W $ is the work done, $ P $ is the pressure, and $ \Delta V $ is the change in volume. Therefore, since $ \Delta V = 0 $, it follows that:

$$ W = 0 $$

This process is also known as an isovolumetric or isometric process and is one of the four main thermodynamic processes that define the conditions under which a gas changes state.

content: Two moles of an ideal gas are compressed isothermally and reversibly from 40 L to 20 L at 300 K . What is the work done? $\left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{W}_{\max } & =-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log _{10} \frac{\mathrm{~V}_2}{\mathrm{~V}_1} \\ & =-2.303 \times 2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \log _{10} \frac{20}{40} \\ & =3457.97 \mathrm{~J} \\ & =3.458 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned}$$

content: Calculate the enthalpy change when 12 g carbon react with sufficient hydrogen to form methane. If enthalpy of formation of methane is $-75 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
explanation:
The reaction under consideration is the formation of methane ($\text{CH}_4$) from carbon ($\text{C}$) and hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$), which can be represented as:

$$ \text{C (s)} + 2\text{H}_2 \text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 \text{(g)} $$

The given standard enthalpy of formation for methane ($\text{CH}_4$) is:

$$ \Delta H_f^\circ = -75 \, \text{kJ} \, \text{mol}^{-1} $$

This value indicates the enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of methane under standard conditions.

To calculate the enthalpy change for the reaction involving 12 g of carbon, follow these steps:

Calculate the number of moles of carbon (C):

The molar mass of carbon is approximately 12 g/mol. Therefore, for 12 g of carbon, the number of moles is:

$$ \text{moles of C} = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}} = \frac{12 \, \text{g}}{12 \, \text{g/mol}} = 1 \, \text{mol} $$

Use the number of moles of carbon to find the enthalpy change:

Since 1 mole of carbon produces 1 mole of methane and the enthalpy of formation of methane is $-75 \, \text{kJ} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$, the total enthalpy change for the reaction is:

$$ \Delta H = -75 \, \text{kJ/mol} \times 1 \, \text{mol} = -75 \, \text{kJ} $$

Thus, the enthalpy change when 12 g of carbon react with sufficient hydrogen to form methane is $-75 \, \text{kJ}$.

Correct answer is Option C: $-75 \mathrm{~kJ}$.

content: Which among the following pair of properties are intensive?
explanation:
Option A, surface tension and viscosity, are both intensive properties.

Explanation:

Intensive Properties: These are properties that do not depend on the size or extent of the system. In other words, intensive properties remain the same regardless of the amount of substance or size of the system.

Surface Tension: This property is intensive because it is independent of the amount of liquid present.

Viscosity: This property is also intensive as it depends on the internal fluid friction and not on the quantity of the fluid.

Contrast with Extensive Properties:

Mass and Internal Energy are extensive properties because they depend on the amount of matter present in the system.

Heat Capacity is extensive, as it depends on the quantity of the substance.

Temperature, Boiling Point, and Specific Heat are intensive properties as they are material properties that do not change with the amount of substance.

Thus, both surface tension and viscosity are intensive properties, which is why Option A is the correct choice.

content: 2 moles of an ideal gas expands isothermally from $5 \mathrm{dm}^3$ to $10 \mathrm{dm}^3$ at a constant external pressure of 1.5 bar. Calculate work done.
explanation:
To calculate the work done by an ideal gas during isothermal expansion at constant external pressure, the formula used is:

$$ W = -P_{\text{ext}} \Delta V $$

where:

$ W $ is the work done by the gas,

$ P_{\text{ext}} $ is the constant external pressure,

$ \Delta V $ is the change in volume.

Given:

$ P_{\text{ext}} = 1.5 \, \text{bar} $,

Initial volume, $ V_i = 5 \, \text{dm}^3 $,

Final volume, $ V_f = 10 \, \text{dm}^3 $.

First, calculate the change in volume:

$$ \Delta V = V_f - V_i = 10 \, \text{dm}^3 - 5 \, \text{dm}^3 = 5 \, \text{dm}^3 $$

Now, plug in the values into the work formula:

$$ W = -1.5 \, \text{bar} \times 5 \, \text{dm}^3 $$

$$ W = -7.5 \, \text{dm}^3 \, \text{bar} $$

Therefore, the work done by the gas is $-7.5 \, \text{dm}^3 \, \text{bar}$.

The correct answer is Option B:

$-7.5 \, \text{dm}^3 \, \text{bar}$

content: An ideal gas expands against constant external pressure of 2 bar from 5 lit to 8 lit and absorbs 10 kJ of heat. What is $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ of the system?
explanation:
$$\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}} \times \Delta \mathrm{V}=-2 \times(8-5)=-6 \mathrm{~L} \text { bar }$$

1 L bar $=100 \mathrm{~J}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore\quad& \mathrm{W}=-600 \mathrm{~J}=-0.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \quad(\because 1 \mathrm{~kJ}=1000 \mathrm{~J}) \\ & \mathrm{Q}=+10 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned}$$

According to the first law of thermodynamics,

$$\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W} \\ \therefore\quad& \Delta \mathrm{U}=+10 \mathrm{~kJ}+(-0.6 \mathrm{~kJ})=9.4 \mathrm{~kJ}=9400 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

content: For an ideal gas, the heat of reaction at constant pressure and heat of reaction at constant volume are related by equation _________
explanation:
The relationship between the heat of reaction at constant pressure ($\Delta H$) and the heat of reaction at constant volume ($\Delta U$) for an ideal gas is given by the equation:

$$ \Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta nRT $$

where $\Delta n$ is the change in the number of moles of gas, $R$ is the ideal gas constant, and $T$ is the temperature in Kelvin. This equation accounts for the work done by the system due to volume change when the reaction occurs at constant pressure, resulting in the addition of the $\Delta nRT$ term. Thus, the correct option is Option D.

content: For the reaction $\mathrm{CH}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_{6(\mathrm{~g})}$ $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=3.356 \times 10^{17}$, calculate $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ for the reaction at 298 K .
explanation:
The relationship between the equilibrium constant, $ K_p $, and the standard Gibbs free energy change, $\Delta G^{\circ}$, for a reaction at a given temperature can be determined by the following equation:

$$ \Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_p $$

where:

$ R $ is the ideal gas constant, $ R = 8.314 \, \text{J/mol} \cdot \text{K} $

$ T $ is the temperature in Kelvin, $ T = 298 \, \text{K} $

$ K_p $ is the equilibrium constant given as $ K_p = 3.356 \times 10^{17} $

Now, let's substitute these values into the equation to find $\Delta G^{\circ}$:

First, calculate $\ln K_p$:

$$ \ln(3.356 \times 10^{17}) \approx 40.344 $$

Substitute the values into the equation:

$$ \Delta G^{\circ} = -(8.314 \, \text{J/mol} \cdot \text{K}) \times (298 \, \text{K}) \times 40.344 $$

Compute the result:

$$ \Delta G^{\circ} = -100401.3 \, \text{J/mol} $$

Convert joules to kilojoules (since the options are in kJ):

$$ \Delta G^{\circ} = -100.401 \, \text{kJ/mol} $$

Therefore, the closest option to this calculation is:

Option B: $-100 \, \text{kJ}$

content: A gas expands isothermally against a constant external pressure of 1 bar from 10 dm$^3$ to 20 dm$^3$ by absorbing 800 J of heat from surrounding. Calculate value of $\Delta$U.
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}}=1 \mathrm{bar} \\ & \mathrm{~V}_1=10 \mathrm{dm}^3, \mathrm{~V}_2=20 \mathrm{dm}^3 \\ & \mathrm{Q}=800 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \mathrm{~W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}}\left(\mathrm{~V}_2-\mathrm{V}_1\right)=-1 \mathrm{bar}\left(20 \mathrm{dm}^3-10 \mathrm{dm}^3\right) \\ & \quad=-10 \mathrm{dm}^3 \text { bar }=-1000 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}=800 \mathrm{~J}-1000 \mathrm{~J}=-200 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

content: Which of the following equations indicates increase in entropy?
explanation: Solid $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ changes to two aqueous products. Aqueous phase has more randomness than solid phase and hence, entropy of the system increases.

content: Which from following statements is true about internal energy?

options.content: It increases with increase in temperature.
It decreases with increase in temperature.
It is not a state function.

explanation:
The true statement about internal energy is:

Option A: It increases with increase in temperature.

Internal energy is the total energy contained within a system due to both microscopic kinetic energy (from molecular motion) and microscopic potential energy (from forces between molecules). As temperature increases, the kinetic energy of the molecules increases because they move faster, which in turn increases the internal energy of the system. Internal energy, represented by $ U $, is indeed a state function, meaning its value for a given state depends solely on the current state of the system and not on how it reached that state.

content: 2 moles of an ideal gas are expanded isothermally and reversibly from 20 L to 40 L at 300 K . Calculate work done. ( $\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$).
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{W}_{\max }=-2.303 \mathrm{~nRT} \log _{10} \frac{\mathrm{~V}_2}{\mathrm{~V}_1} \\ & =-2.303 \times 2 \mathrm{~mol} \times 8.314 \mathrm{~JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K}^2 \log _{10} \frac{40}{20} \\ & =-2.303 \times 2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 0.3010 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~W}_{\max }=-3,457.97 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

content: 10 g each of $\mathrm{NH}_3, \mathrm{~N}_2, \mathrm{Cl}_2$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ are expanded isothermally and reversibly at same temperature. Identify gas that performs maximum work.
explanation:
$$\mathrm{W}_{\max }=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log _{10} \frac{\mathrm{~V}_2}{\mathrm{~V}_1}$$

Hence, $\mathrm{W}_{\text {max }} \propto \mathrm{n}$

(Given: $\mathrm{R}, \mathrm{T}, \mathrm{V}_2, \mathrm{~V}_1=$ Constant)

$$\mathrm{W}_{\max } \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{M} \cdot \mathrm{~W}} \text { (Given: equal mass) }$$

Hence, lower the molecular mass, greater is the work done. Among the given, $\mathrm{NH}_3$ has the lowest molecular mass which performs maximum work.

content: Identify the factor from following on which heat of reaction does not depend.

options.content: Physical states of reactants and products.
Total number of steps in which reaction occurs.
Whether the reaction is carried out at constant pressure or constant volume.

explanation:
The heat of reaction does not depend on the total number of steps in which the reaction occurs. This principle is derived from Hess's Law, which states that the total enthalpy change for a chemical reaction is the same, regardless of whether it occurs in one step or multiple steps.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option B: Total number of steps in which reaction occurs.

content:
For the reaction,

$$2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}, \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-573.2 \mathrm{~kJ}$$

What is heat of decomposition of water per mol?
explanation:
The given reaction is:

$$2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\eta)} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-573.2 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$

Reversing the above reaction,

$$2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(t)} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \Delta_{\mathrm{d}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=573.2 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$

$\therefore \quad$ For decomposition of 1 mole of water,

$$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{d}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=286.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$

content:
For the reaction,

$$\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_{8(\mathrm{~g})}+5 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})}$$

at constant temperature, $\Delta \mathrm{H}-\Delta \mathrm{U}$ is
explanation:
The difference between the change in enthalpy ($\Delta H$) and the change in internal energy ($\Delta U$) for a reaction occurring at constant temperature and pressure is given by:

$$ \Delta H - \Delta U = \Delta n_{\text{gas}} \cdot R \cdot T $$

where:

$\Delta n_{\text{gas}}$ is the change in moles of gas during the reaction,

$R$ is the ideal gas constant,

$T$ is the temperature in Kelvin.

For the given reaction:

$$ \mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_{8(\mathrm{~g})} + 5 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} + 4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} $$

Count the moles of gaseous reactants and products:

Gaseous reactants: $1 \text{ mole of } \mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_8 + 5 \text{ moles of } \mathrm{O}_2 = 6 \text{ moles of gas}$

Gaseous products: $3 \text{ moles of } \mathrm{CO}_2$

The change in moles of gas, $\Delta n_{\text{gas}}$, is:

$$ \Delta n_{\text{gas}} = \text{moles of gaseous products} - \text{moles of gaseous reactants} = 3 - 6 = -3 $$

Substitute $\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = -3$ into the equation for $\Delta H - \Delta U$:

$$ \Delta H - \Delta U = (-3) \cdot R \cdot T = -3RT $$

The correct answer is Option C, which corresponds to $-3RT$.

content: What is change in internal energy of the system when work done by the system is 150 J and system release 300 J of heat?
explanation:
According to the first law of thermodynamics,

$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}=-300 \mathrm{~J}-150 \mathrm{~J}=-450 \mathrm{~J}$.

content: Two moles of an ideal gas is expanded isothermally from a volume of $300 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ to 2.5 $\mathrm{dm}^3$ at 298 K against a constant pressure at 1.9 bar. Calculate the work done in joules.
explanation:
To calculate the work done when an ideal gas expands isothermally against a constant pressure, the formula to use is:

$$ W = -P_{\text{ext}} \Delta V $$

where:

$ W $ is the work done by the gas (negative when the gas does work on the surroundings).

$ P_{\text{ext}} $ is the external pressure (in pascals).

$ \Delta V $ is the change in volume (in cubic meters).

Step-by-Step Calculation:

Convert Volumes to Appropriate Units:

Initial volume: $ V_1 = 300 \, \mathrm{cm}^3 = 300 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m}^3 = 0.0003 \, \mathrm{m}^3 $

Final volume: $ V_2 = 2.5 \, \mathrm{dm}^3 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}^3 = 0.0025 \, \mathrm{m}^3 $

Calculate the Change in Volume:

$$ \Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = 0.0025 \, \mathrm{m}^3 - 0.0003 \, \mathrm{m}^3 = 0.0022 \, \mathrm{m}^3 $$

Convert Pressure to Pascals:

The pressure is given as $ 1.9 \, \text{bar} $. Convert this to pascals knowing that $ 1 \, \text{bar} = 10^5 \, \text{Pa} $.

$$ P_{\text{ext}} = 1.9 \, \text{bar} = 1.9 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} $$

Calculate the Work Done:

Using the formula for work:

$$ W = -P_{\text{ext}} \Delta V = -(1.9 \times 10^5 \, \mathrm{Pa})(0.0022 \, \mathrm{m}^3) $$

$$ W = -(1.9 \times 10^5 \times 0.0022) \, \mathrm{J} $$

$$ W = -418 \, \mathrm{J} $$

The work done during the isothermal expansion of the gas is $-418 \, \mathrm{J}$. Therefore, the correct answer is Option A: $-418 \, \mathrm{J}$.

content:
Calculate heat required to convert 9 g of liquid water to water vapours from following equations.

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})} \Delta \mathrm{H}=-57 \mathrm{kCal} \\ & \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \Delta \mathrm{H}=-68.3 \mathrm{kCal} \end{aligned}$$
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})} & \Delta \mathrm{H}=-57 \mathrm{kcal} \\ \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} & \Delta \mathrm{H}=68.3 \mathrm{kcal} \end{array}$$

In the second equation, the given reaction for liquid water is reversed. So, the sign of $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ changes from $-68.3$ kcal to $+68.3$ kcal.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Adding both equations } \\ & \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})} \\ \therefore \quad & \Delta \mathrm{H}=-57+68.3 \mathrm{kcal}=11.3 \mathrm{kcal} \\ & \text { Heat required for } 1 \mathrm{~mole}(18 \mathrm{~g})=11.3 \mathrm{~kcal} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Heat required for } 0.5 \mathrm{~mole}(9 \mathrm{~g})=11.3 \times 0.5 \mathrm{~kcal} \\ & =5.65 \mathrm{~kcal} \end{aligned}$$

This means $18 \, \mathrm{g}$ of water needs $11.3$ kcal to change from liquid to vapour. Therefore, $9 \, \mathrm{g}$ of water needs $5.65$ kcal.

content: One mole of a gas occupying 3 L volume is expanded against a constant external pressure of 1 bar to a volume of 15 L . Calculate work done by the system
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{V}_1 & =3 \mathrm{~L}=3 \mathrm{dm}^3 \\ \mathrm{~V}_2 & =15 \mathrm{~L}=15 \mathrm{dm}^3 \\ \mathrm{~W} & =-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}} \Delta \mathrm{~V} \\ & =-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}}\left(\mathrm{~V}_2-\mathrm{V}_1\right) \\ & =-1 \mathrm{bar}\left(15 \mathrm{dm}^3-3 \mathrm{dm}^3\right) \\ & =-12 \mathrm{dm}^3 \mathrm{bar} \\ & =-12 \mathrm{dm}^3 \mathrm{bar} \times 100 \frac{\mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{dm}^3 \mathrm{bar}} \\ & =-1.200 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

content: Which from the following defines enthalpy of a system?
explanation:
Enthalpy of a system is defined as the sum of the internal energy ($U$) and the product of pressure ($P$) and volume ($V$). This can be expressed mathematically as:

$$ H = U + PV $$

Therefore, the correct definition of enthalpy is given by Option C: Internal energy ($U$) + $PV$.

Enthalpy is a thermodynamic property that is particularly useful in dealing with processes that occur at constant pressure, such as chemical reactions occurring in open containers. Its unit is typically joules (J) in the International System of Units (SI).

content: Which of the following symbols represent heat of reaction at constant volume?
explanation:
According to the first law of thermodynamics,

$$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{Q}-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \cdot \Delta \mathrm{V}\left(\because \mathrm{~W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \cdot \Delta \mathrm{V}\right)$$

For an isochoric process: $\Delta \mathrm{V}=0$

$\therefore$ Heat of reaction $=q_v=\Delta U$

content: Calculate the entropy change for melting 1 g ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in $\mathrm{Jg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ if heat of fusion of ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $80 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$.
explanation:
To calculate the entropy change for melting ice, we use the formula for entropy change ($\Delta S$) when a substance undergoes a phase change:

$$ \Delta S = \frac{q_{\text{rev}}}{T} $$

where $q_{\text{rev}}$ is the heat absorbed or released during the process, and $T$ is the temperature in Kelvin.

Given data:

Heat of fusion of ice, $q_{\text{rev}} = 80 \, \text{J/g}$

Temperature, $T = 0^\circ \text{C} = 273.15 \, \text{K}$

Plug the values into the entropy change formula:

$$ \Delta S = \frac{80 \, \text{J/g}}{273.15 \, \text{K}} $$

Calculating the above:

$$ \Delta S \approx \frac{80}{273.15} \approx 0.293 \, \text{J/g K} $$

Therefore, the entropy change for melting 1 g of ice at $0^\circ \text{C}$ is approximately $0.293 \, \text{J/g K}$. The correct option is B.

content: Which of the following set of properties is correct when one mole of a gas is heated keeping volume constant by increasing temperature and supplying 500 J of heat?
explanation:
Heat is supplied to the system.

$$\therefore \quad q=500 J$$

At constant volume, $\Delta \mathrm{V}=0$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{w}=-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}} \Delta \mathrm{~V}=0$$

According to the first law of thermodynamics,

$$\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{U} =\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{w} \\ & \therefore \quad \Delta \mathrm{U} =500+0=500 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

content:
Find value of Q from following equations.

(i) $\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{QkJ}$

(ii) $\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(8)} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(8)} \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{x} \mathrm{kJ}$

(iii) $\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~s})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{ykJ}$
explanation:
To find the value of $ Q $, analyze the given system of equations from thermodynamics. The equations provided are reactions with associated enthalpy changes ($ \Delta \mathrm{H} $) that describe the energy change for the respective reactions:

$\mathrm{C_{(s)}} + \mathrm{O_{2(g)}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO_{2(g)}} \quad \Delta \mathrm{H} = \mathrm{QkJ}$

$\mathrm{C_{(s)}} + \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O_{2}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO_{2}} \quad \Delta \mathrm{H} = -\mathrm{x\ kJ}$

$\mathrm{C_{(s)}} + \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O_{2}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO_{2}} \quad \Delta \mathrm{H} = -\mathrm{ykJ}$

From the equations given, compare reactions (ii) and (iii):

Reaction (ii) is forming one mole of $\mathrm{CO}$ from half a mole of $\mathrm{O}_2$.

Reaction (iii) provides an enthalpy change for forming $\mathrm{CO}$ from another instance, under different conditions potentially based on the notation.

Using Hess's law, which states that the total enthalpy change for a reaction is the same regardless of the route taken, we can deduce:

For reaction 1 (as given):

$ \mathrm{C_{(s)}} + \mathrm{O_{2(g)}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO_{2(g)}} \quad \Delta \mathrm{H} = Q $

Reactions (ii) and (iii) combined can represent the full reaction in equation (i):

$ \left(\mathrm{C_{(s)}} + \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O_{2}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}\right) + \left(\mathrm{CO} + \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O_{2}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO_{2}}\right) $

This is essentially adding the two half reactions to form the reaction in equation (i):

The combined reactions show:

$ Q = \Delta \mathrm{H}_{ii} + \Delta \mathrm{H}_{iii} = -x - y $

Thus, the value of $\mathrm{Q}$ is $- (x + y)$ kJ. This corresponds to Option A:

$ \text{Option A: } -(x+y) \text{ kJ} $

content: Which of the following statements is appropriate as per first law of thermodynamics?

options.content: Energy of the system remains constant.
Energy of the surrounding remains constant.
Entropy of the universe remains constant.
Energy of the universe remains constant.

explanation:
The appropriate statement according to the first law of thermodynamics is:

Option D: Energy of the universe remains constant.

The first law of thermodynamics, also known as the law of energy conservation, states that energy cannot be created or destroyed in an isolated system. Instead, it can only be transformed from one form to another. Mathematically, this law can be expressed as:

$$ \Delta U = Q - W $$

where:

$\Delta U$ is the change in internal energy of the system,

$Q$ is the heat added to the system,

$W$ is the work done by the system.

This relationship implies that the total energy of the universe (comprising both the system and its surroundings) remains constant. Thus, Option D correctly captures the essence of the first law of thermodynamics.

content:
Given that $$\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}+4 \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4(\mathrm{g})} \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=-1665 \mathrm{~kJ}$$

What is bond energy per mole of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond?
explanation:
To determine the bond energy per mole of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond, we need to analyze the given reaction:

$$ \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})} + 4 \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4(\mathrm{g})} $$

with an enthalpy change of:

$$ \Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ} = -1665 \mathrm{~kJ} $$

This indicates the formation of one mole of $\mathrm{CH}_4$ from its gaseous atoms. Since one mole of methane ($\mathrm{CH}_4$) contains four $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds, the total energy released in forming these bonds is 1665 kJ.

The bond energy per mole of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ can be calculated by dividing the total enthalpy change by the number of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds:

$$ \text{Bond energy per mole of } \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H} = \frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}}{4} = \frac{-1665 \mathrm{~kJ}}{4} $$

Calculating this gives:

$$ = -416.25 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $$

However, the energy of formation is typically discussed in terms of bond formation, which is positive per convention. Therefore, the absolute value is taken:

$$ \text{Bond energy per mole of } \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H} = 416 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: $416 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

content: If bond formation energy of $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is $-433 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ find the bond dissociation energy for 0.5 mole $H_{2(g)}$.
explanation:
For the given reaction,

$$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{g})}, \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=433 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$

(Bond dissociation energy for 1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2(g)}$ $=-\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}$ bond formation energy)

$\therefore \quad$ Bond dissociation energy for 0.5 moles of $\mathrm{H}_2=0.5 \times 433 \mathrm{~kJ}=216.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$

content: What is the value of standard enthalpy of formation of dihydrogen?
explanation:
The standard enthalpy of formation of a substance is defined as the change in enthalpy when one mole of the substance is formed from its elements in their standard states under standard conditions (typically 1 atm pressure and 298 K).

For diatomic molecules like dihydrogen ($\text{H}_2$), which is in its standard state, the standard enthalpy of formation is zero. This is because dihydrogen is already in its most stable form under standard conditions.

Therefore, the value of the standard enthalpy of formation of dihydrogen ($\text{H}_2$) is:

Option B:

$$ 0 \, \text{kJ/mol} $$

content: Calculate Gibbs energy change for a reaction having $\Delta \mathrm{H}=31400 \mathrm{~J}, \Delta \mathrm{~S}=32 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{T} & =1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1273 \mathrm{~K} \\ \Delta \mathrm{G} & =\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{~S} \\ & =31400 \mathrm{~J}-1273 \mathrm{~K}\left(32 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}\right) \\ & =31400 \mathrm{~J}-40736 \mathrm{~J} \\ \Delta \mathrm{G} & =-9336 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

content: If 100 L gas is enclosed in a cylinder, absorbs 302.6 J of heat and expands to 200 L against constant external pressure of 2 atm . Calculate internal energy change of the gas.
